author: Clement of Rome
interpretation: Clement echoes the Ephesians 2 logic when he writes that the patriarchs, apostles, and all the righteous were 'adorned with good works' but 'were made perfect not through their own works but through the will of God.' Righteousness is the fruit, not the root, of salvation. Clement seamlessly connects the gift character of salvation with the expectation of transformed behavior, showing no tension between grace and obedience.
context: Clement's letter is among the earliest non-canonical Christian writings and shows how the first generation after the apostles read texts like Ephesians 2:8–9. The integration of grace and works without systematic distinction anticipates how the Eastern tradition would continue to read Paul.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom emphasizes the completeness of grace: salvation is 'not of yourselves' because even faith is God's gift, not a human contribution that would give grounds for boasting. He interprets 'not of works' as excluding any pre-conversion merit but immediately points to verse 10 ('created in Christ Jesus for good works') to show that grace produces, rather than eliminates, moral effort. Good works are the purpose of salvation, not its cause.
context: Chrysostom's reading harmonizes grace and works by temporal ordering: grace precedes and enables works. This approach became characteristic of Eastern Christianity, which typically avoids the sharp grace/works dichotomy of later Western debates.

author: Augustine of Hippo
source_title: On the Predestination of the Saints
interpretation: Augustine used Ephesians 2:8–9 as the decisive proof text against Pelagius. The 'this' that is 'not of yourselves' refers not merely to salvation in general but to faith itself. Even the capacity to believe is a gift of grace; otherwise, the person who believed could boast of having done something that the unbeliever did not. Grace is therefore entirely prevenient: it precedes, causes, and sustains every movement of the will toward God.
context: Augustine's reading of 'and this not of yourselves' as referring to faith became the cornerstone of Western predestinarian theology. The grammatical question — whether 'this' (touto) refers to faith, grace, or the entire preceding clause — remains debated, but Augustine's theological conclusion shaped a millennium of Western soteriology.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas reads the passage within his framework of habitual grace: God infuses the supernatural virtue of faith into the soul, enabling the person to assent to revealed truth. This infused faith is emphatically 'the gift of God' because it exceeds the natural capacity of the human intellect. Works done after receiving habitual grace do merit further grace and glory, but only because the grace empowering them is itself gratuitous. The exclusion of boasting applies to the starting point of salvation, not to the entire process.
context: Aquinas's interpretation preserves Augustine's emphasis on prevenient grace while carving out space for grace-empowered merit — the position the Council of Trent would defend against the Reformers. Luther and Calvin would argue this framework effectively reintroduced works through the back door.

author: Martin Luther (contra Erasmus)
interpretation: In his debate with Erasmus, Luther wielded Ephesians 2:8–9 to argue for the bondage of the will: if salvation is by grace through faith and 'not of yourselves,' then the human will contributes nothing to conversion. Erasmus had argued for a small role for free will in accepting grace; Luther responded that even this small role would give grounds for boasting, which Paul explicitly excludes. The will is either bound to sin (before grace) or liberated by grace — it is never neutral or self-determining in spiritual matters.
context: The Luther-Erasmus debate over free will (1524–1525) was one of the pivotal exchanges of the Reformation. Luther considered The Bondage of the Will his most important theological work. Erasmus's counter-argument — that grace initiates but the will cooperates — anticipated the later Arminian position.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin argues that 'this' in 'and this not of yourselves' must refer to faith, not merely to salvation in general, because Paul's purpose is to eliminate every ground for human boasting. If faith were a human decision, it would become a work by another name — the one thing the sinner contributes to their own salvation. Calvin insists faith is the instrument, not the cause, of justification: it is the empty hand that receives Christ's righteousness, contributing nothing of its own.
context: Calvin's metaphor of faith as an 'empty hand' became standard Reformed language for distinguishing the instrumental role of faith from the meritorious cause of justification (Christ's work). This formulation guards against treating faith itself as a work while affirming its necessity.

author: Karl Barth
interpretation: Barth reads Ephesians 2:8–9 within his christological framework: the grace by which we are saved is not an abstract divine attribute but the concrete person Jesus Christ. To be 'saved by grace' is to be incorporated into the humanity of the One who has already been judged and justified on behalf of all. Faith is the human acknowledgment of what is already true in Christ; it does not create a new reality but recognizes the reality God has established in the cross and resurrection.
context: Barth's reading raises the question of whether grace is universally effective (apokatastasis) or requires personal appropriation. Barth officially declined to affirm universalism but his theology pushes strongly in that direction, as many interpreters have noted.

author: N.T. Wright
source_title: Paul and the Faithfulness of God
interpretation: Wright reads Ephesians 2:8–9 in its immediate context: the 'you' are Gentiles who have been brought near to God's covenant people (2:11–13). The 'works' excluded are not generic moral effort but Torah-observance as the entry requirement for the people of God. Grace means that Gentiles are included in God's family without becoming Jewish. Wright insists this is not a general theory about how individuals get saved but a specific statement about the basis of Gentile inclusion in the Messiah's people.
context: Wright's reading of Ephesians 2:8–9 as primarily about Jew-Gentile inclusion rather than individual salvation has been controversial. Critics argue he narrows the passage's application; Wright responds that the Reformation reading decontextualizes Paul's argument from its original ecclesiological setting.

author: Thomas Schreiner
source_title: Paul, Apostle of God's Glory in Christ
interpretation: Schreiner defends the traditional reading: the 'works' excluded in verse 9 cannot be limited to Jewish identity markers because Paul's argument in Ephesians 2:1–7 describes the universal human condition of spiritual death, not a specifically Jewish problem. All people — Jew and Gentile — are 'dead in trespasses and sins' and require God's unilateral act of making them alive. The exclusion of boasting (v. 9) applies to every conceivable form of human contribution to salvation.
context: Schreiner represents the careful evangelical scholarship that engages New Perspective arguments at the exegetical level rather than simply reasserting confessional positions. His reading of Ephesians 2:1–9 as describing universal anthropology before addressing Jew-Gentile relations (2:11ff) has significant textual support.
